steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses (ICs) joined by the conjunctive adverb “however”: “In many languages, including English, these consonants are ______” and “in some dialects of Mandarin, however, four such consonants…are relatively common.” When a conjunctive adverb like “however” joins two ICs, a semicolon must end the first clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘rare and’: adding “and” before “however” is grammatically redundant and does not supply the required punctuation between two ICs. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘rare;’: the semicolon correctly separates the two independent clauses; “however” then begins the second clause as a conjunctive adverb. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘rare’: no punctuation between two ICs creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘rare,’: a comma alone between two ICs joined only by a conjunctive adverb creates a comma splice. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
